original_abstract:
Modern hybrid armed conflicts, specifically the ongoing Special Military Operation, have presented the medical services of the Armed Forces with an array of complex challenges. This review examines international literature—primarily focusing on NATO perspectives—to identify the critical factors influencing military medical outcomes today. The analysis reveals that operational success is fundamentally constrained by the staffing, qualitative expertise, and specialized readiness of medical personnel. A recurring "peacetime effect" highlights the difficulty of maintaining perishable surgical and resuscitative skills necessary for unique wartime pathologies.

Beyond personnel, the integrity of medical infrastructure is pivotal; the review notes that modern hybrid strategies often result in targeted attacks on medical facilities and necessitate the utilization of local infrastructure in newly held or hostile territories. In terms of clinical outcomes, prehospital care remains the primary bottleneck for reducing mortality. Fatal hemorrhaging and head injuries resulting from mine-blast trauma are the leading causes of death on the battlefield. The data suggests that timely tourniquet application, rapid evacuation, and early blood transfusions provide the most significant contributions to survival.

While mine-blast (61.4–83.5%) and head injuries (20.9–59.0%) are the most lethal, limb injuries (45.7%) represent the most significant long-term burden. These wounds often result in years lived with disability that exceed those of major chronic conditions like cancer or cardiovascular disease, making them a focal point for military medical efforts. Finally, the review notes a lack of international consensus regarding the optimal echelon and location for providing qualified and specialized surgical care, indicating that medical strategies must remain flexible to adapt to the shifting nature of hybrid warfare.
humanized_abstract:
In the conditions of modern hybrid armed conflicts, including the Special Military Operation, the military medical service of the Armed Forces of the Russian Federation has to solve a wide range of problems. This systematic review of the foreign and Russian scientific literature on the issues of military medicine, using primarily the NATO standards and approaches, reveals that the current capabilities and outcomes of military medical service are significantly influenced by a whole range of factors including human resources, professionalism of personnel and operational readiness. In particular, it is confirmed that the very ability to conduct and complete successful military operations largely depends on the strengths, qualifications and degree of operational readiness of healthcare personnel and medical capabilities in the time and space of the combat operation. One of the factors, confirmed in existing literature, is the so-called "peacetime effect" of the loss of a whole range of short-term skills necessary for the implementation of professional activities in the context of solving wartime problems and caring for war-injured patients.

Staff and facilities are crucial to the practice of medicine. Modern hybrid warfare tactics have increased the intention to destroy medical facilities. Furthermore, local resources may be required to be used in newly-occupied or enemy controlled areas. Prehospital care is currently the main rate limiting factor in reducing deaths on the battlefield. Fatal haemorrhagic and head wounds due to mine-blast trauma are the common causes of death in the wounded on the battlefield. Improving survival by increasing the number of patients being successfully treated in hospitals in the region in a timely fashion will depend on a number of factors, however it appears that tourniquet application, rapid transportation to a hospital and blood transfusions are of the greatest benefit to patients suffering from such injuries.

In hybride oorlogszetting vormen mine-blast injuries (61.4–83.5%) en hoofdwonden (20.9–59.0%) de meest lethal vorm van wond Let alone, limb injuries (45.7%) constituted the highest long-term burden. The years lived with disability due to limb injuries greatly exceed those of many major chronic conditions like cancer and cardiovascular disease making this a priority in military medicine. Finally, the literature review highlighted the inconsistency in the international literature as regards the most appropriate level and location for safe and specialist surgical care in Hybrid Warfare. Something that may change depending on the specific context of the war being waged.
